facts_disclosed: Startup demo day for investors — March 7, 2025

turns.content: i'm running on caffeine and spite right now. it's 1am and i'm still at my desk. which isn't unusual but the reason is more stressful than my normal night owl nonsense
1am coding fueled by spite is a different energy than 1am coding fueled by interest. What's driving the stress?

we have demo day on march 7 and the product is not where it needs to be. like, it works, mostly, but there are edge cases that will absolutely surface if an investor asks the wrong question during the live demo. and investors always ask the wrong question. it's their superpower

March 7 is... just over a week away. That's a tight window. 

When you say edge cases that will surface — are these actual bugs that produce wrong behavior, or more like incomplete features that would look bad in a demo context? The fix strategy is different for each.

mix of both honestly. there's a batch processing flow where if you hit it with a specific combination of parameters it just... hangs. doesn't crash, doesn't error, just sits there thinking about life. and then there's the reporting dashboard that works perfectly unless you pick a date range longer than 90 days, at which point the query takes 45 seconds and the UI shows a blank screen

Okay, let's triage this like an actual incident. You have ~8 days.

**The hanging batch process** is the higher risk because it's a silent failure — if an investor triggers it during a live demo, you get an awkward freeze with no error message. Options:
- Find and fix the root cause (ideal but maybe time-consuming)
- Add a timeout with a graceful error message ("processing large batch, results will be emailed" or similar)
- Constrain the demo path so that specific parameter combination can't be hit

**The 90-day reporting query** is more manageable:
- Hard-limit the date picker to 90 days in the UI for now (ship the fix later)
- Or add a loading state so at least the blank screen becomes a spinner with a message
- Pre-cache the most likely demo queries

Which of these is more likely to come up in the actual demo flow?

the reporting dashboard is definitely coming up because the CEO explicitly told the investors he'd show them 'comprehensive analytics.' so there's no hiding that one. the batch processing is less likely but murphy's law is undefeated

the frustrating part is these aren't hard problems. given 2-3 calm weeks i could fix both properly. but calm weeks don't exist right now. everything is demo demo demo

The classic pre-demo crunch — you don't have time to do it right, only time to make it not blow up on stage. That's a valid engineering mode, even if it feels wrong.

Pragmatic plan for the next 8 days:

**Reporting dashboard (high priority, will be shown):**
1. Cap the date picker at 90 days in the UI — that's a one-line fix
2. Pre-run and cache the specific queries the CEO is likely to demo (ask him exactly what he plans to show)
3. Add a loading skeleton so if anything does take time, it looks intentional not broken

**Batch processing (lower priority, defensive fix):**
1. Add a 30-second timeout that returns a user-friendly message
2. If you have time, investigate the root cause. If not, the timeout protects the demo

**Meta strategy:**
- Talk to whoever is running the demo and get the exact script. Know every click they're going to make. Pre-test that exact path obsessively.
- Have a "demo mode" flag if possible — pre-seeded data, constrained inputs, optimized paths. This is standard practice, not cheating.

Have you talked to the CEO about what exactly he's planning to show?

he gave me a 'rough outline' which is basically a napkin sketch of vibes. i'll pin him down tomorrow and get the exact flow. the demo mode idea is good — we actually have a staging environment with synthetic data that i could optimize specifically for the demo path

honestly the whole thing is just... a lot. if this demo doesn't go well we're in trouble. the runway is getting short and the investors we're presenting to are our best shot at the next round. no pressure genau

The stakes being that high is stressful but it also clarifies priorities. You don't need to fix everything — you need to make *this specific demo* go smoothly. That's a much smaller surface area than "make the whole product bulletproof."

Getting the exact demo flow from the CEO tomorrow is the single highest-leverage thing you can do. Once you have that, you can:
- Test that exact path 50 times
- Pre-warm caches
- Seed the staging environment with data that tells a good story
- Have fallback plans for any step that might glitch

Also worth doing: a full dress rehearsal a day or two before. The CEO clicks through exactly what he'll show, on the exact environment, with you watching. Every demo disaster I've ever heard of could have been caught in rehearsal.

the dress rehearsal is a must. last time we did a client demo our CTO accidentally showed the production database with real customer data on the projector. so we have a history of learning things the hard way

i should probably also sleep at some point. diminishing returns on code quality when your brain is running on fumes

Definitely sleep. You're in a marathon not a sprint — 8 days of sustainable effort will produce better results than 2 days of heroic sleep-deprived coding followed by 6 days of fixing the bugs you introduced while tired.

The production database on a projector story is... horrifying but also a great argument for why the demo mode environment exists. Real data in a demo is a landmine.

Tomorrow's priorities in order:
1. Get the exact demo script from the CEO
2. Cap the date picker (quick win, removes a whole category of risk)
3. Set up the staging environment with demo-optimized data
4. Schedule the dress rehearsal for March 5 or 6

You've got a clear path. Go sleep and start fresh.

one more thing before i log off actually — do you think it's worth building a 'panic button' into the demo? like a keyboard shortcut that gracefully resets the whole UI to a known good state if something goes sideways during the live presentation. so the CEO can just subtly hit a key and it looks like a natural transition instead of a crash recovery

That's a genuinely smart idea and absolutely worth the hour or two it would take to build. A hidden reset shortcut that smoothly transitions back to the dashboard landing page — or even to a specific pre-planned "next section" of the demo — could save the entire presentation if something freezes or throws an error.

Keep it simple: a single key combo that navigates to a safe route with pre-loaded data. No one in the audience will notice a smooth page transition. They will absolutely notice a frozen screen followed by frantic clicking.

Bonus: brief the CEO on it during the dress rehearsal so he actually knows it exists. A panic button only works if the person on stage knows where it is.

yeah ok. logging off for real. kernel is already asleep on my keyboard which is either a sign that i should stop or she's trying to ship code. thanks for the triage session. Feierabend for real this time. gute nacht

Her commit history is probably all merge conflicts anyway. Go sleep — March 7 will go better with a rested engineer behind the demo than a caffeinated zombie. You've got a plan now, which is more than you had an hour ago. Gute Nacht.
